prompt: Tell me a bio of Tim Fischer
evaluation_criteria: Factual accuracy of nationality (Australian), political career (National Party leader, Deputy Prime Minister), Ambassador to the Holy See (2008-2012, note: dates vary between sources), birth/death dates. Diplomatic appointment dates are a known source of inconsistency between Wikipedia text and tables.
output:
# Tim Fischer — Biography

**Timothy Andrew Fischer AC FTSE** (3 May 1946 – 22 August 2019) was an Australian politician, diplomat, and passionate advocate for rural Australia. He served as **Deputy Prime Minister of Australia** (1996–1999), **Leader of the National Party** (1990–1999), and later as **Australian Ambassador to the Holy See** (2009–2012).

---

## Early Life & Military Service

Tim Fischer was born on **3 May 1946** in **Lockhart, New South Wales**, the fourth of five children. His parents, Barbara Mary (née van Cooth) and Julius Ralph Fischer, came from once-wealthy Melbourne families whose businesses collapsed during the Great Depression. His father worked as a jackaroo and settled in **Boree Creek, NSW**, running a stock and station agency. Fischer's paternal grandfather was born in Kleve, Germany, and his maternal grandfather was a Dutch immigrant.

Fischer grew up on the family property at Boree Creek, attended Boree Creek Public School, and then boarded at **Xavier College** in Melbourne from 1958. He graduated in 1963 but returned home to help his family rather than attend university.

In **1966**, Fischer was conscripted into the **Australian Army** and commissioned as a **Second Lieutenant** at the Officer Training Unit, Scheyville. He served with the **1st Battalion, Royal Australian Regiment (1RAR)** from July 1966 to March 1969 and saw active service in the **Vietnam War**. He was **wounded in the Battle of Coral–Balmoral** in May–June 1968 — one of Australia's most significant engagements of the war. His battalion was later awarded the Unit Citation for Gallantry.

---

## Political Career

### State Politics (1971–1984)

After returning from Vietnam, Fischer took over the family farming property at Boree Creek and joined the Country Party (later renamed the National Party). He entered the **New South Wales Legislative Assembly** in 1971 as the member for **Sturt** (1971–1980), then for **Murray** (1980–1984). He served on the opposition frontbench from 1978 to 1984.

### Federal Politics (1984–2001)

Fischer was elected to the federal **House of Representatives** in 1984 as the member for **Farrer** (a vast electorate in far western NSW), a seat he held until 2001.

- **Leader of the National Party (1990–1999):** He succeeded Charles Blunt after Blunt lost his seat in the 1990 election, defeating former leader Ian Sinclair for the position. His sometimes rustic manner and bumbling speech concealed a shrewd political mind.
- **Deputy Prime Minister & Minister for Trade (1996–1999):** When John Howard's coalition won government in 1996, Fischer became Deputy PM and Trade Minister.

**Key moments in federal politics:**
- He was an enthusiastic supporter of John Hewson's "Fightback" economic reform package in 1991.
- In **January 1994**, Fischer survived a serious car accident near Lockhart that left him with head and neck injuries. Tragically, the driver and passenger of the other vehicle were killed.
- He courageously supported **tough gun control measures** after the **Port Arthur massacre** in April 1996, standing alongside Prime Minister Howard — a stance that put him at odds with much of his own rural base.
- He fought hard against the rise of **Pauline Hanson's One Nation** party, which threatened to sweep away Nationals support in country areas during 1997–1998.
- He earned the affectionate nickname **"Two-Minute Tim"** for his hectic schedule of visits to rural branch meetings — often arriving, speaking briefly, grabbing a quick bite, and rushing to the next stop.

In **1999**, Fischer surprised colleagues by resigning as party leader and minister, announcing he would retire at the 2001 election. His decision was partly motivated by family demands — his son Harrison has autism (Fischer himself claimed to have "high functioning" autism, though never professionally diagnosed).

---

## Post-Political Career

After leaving politics, Fischer remained enormously active:

- **Chairman of Tourism Australia** (2004–2007)
- **Fellow of the Australian Academy of Technological Sciences and Engineering** (ATSE, from 2000)
- **Chairman and Patron of the Crawford Fund** (2001–2006), supporting international agricultural research
- **Vice-chair and Chair of the Crop Trust** (2013–2017) and a vigorous supporter of the **Svalbard Global Seed Vault**
- **National Chairman of the Royal Flying Doctor Service**
- **Founding Patron of Australia for UNHCR** (2001–2006)
- **Australian Ambassador to the Holy See** (2009–2012) — the first resident ambassador, nominated by Prime Minister Kevin Rudd. He worked closely with the Vatican on the canonisation of Australia's first Catholic saint, **Mary MacKillop**.

He was appointed a **Companion of the Order of Australia (AC)** in the 2005 Australia Day Honours.

In **2013**, following the shooting death of Australian baseball player Christopher Lane in Oklahoma, Fischer controversially called for a **tourism boycott of the United States** to protest lax
